title: Vibrational model of entropy in dense two-dimensional fluids
abstract: A vibrational paradigm of atomic dynamic in dense fluids is known to provide useful insight on the transport and thermodynamic properties of fluids in three dimensions. In this paper, a vibrational model is generalized to describe the excess entropy of two-dimensional (2D) fluids. A simple practical implementation of this model is demonstrated to deliver accurate results for various systems, such as one-component plasmas with Coulomb and logarithmic interactions, a 2D fluid of dipole particles, and a 2D Yukawa fluid. The applicability limits, relevance to three-dimensional fluids, relations to other 2D phenomena, and potential practical applications are briefly discussed.
summary: This paper develops and applies a vibrational model to calculate the excess entropy of dense two-dimensional fluids, extending a known 3D technique to 2D systems. It successfully tests this generalized model against various 2D plasmas and fluids, showing strong agreement with simulation results. The work provides a powerful theoretical tool for characterizing the thermodynamics of strongly coupled 2D matter.
main_problem: The paper generalizes a vibrational model of entropy, previously used for 3D dense fluids, to accurately calculate the excess entropy of two-dimensional (2D) fluids.
main_result: The derived vibrational model provides a universal and simple generic form for the excess entropy ($s_{ex}$) that shows good agreement with numerical simulation data for various 2D systems.
method: The excess entropy is calculated using a vibrational paradigm, approximating the partition function and relating $s_{ex}$ to the excess thermal energy and free energy via established thermodynamic relations.
model_or_system: The study focuses on dense 2D fluids, including one-component plasmas (OCP) with Coulomb and logarithmic interactions, 2D dipole fluids, and 2D Yukawa fluids.
key_observables: Excess entropy ($s_{ex}$), excess thermal internal energy ($u_{th}$), and the coupling parameter ($\Gamma$).
important_parameters: Coupling parameter ($\Gamma$), characteristic frequency ($\omega_E$), and plasma frequency ($\omega_0$).
main_assumptions: The model relies on the Einstein approximation for the vibrational density of states, and the derived equations are most accurate in the strongly coupled regime ($\Gamma \gg 1$).
figures_summary: Figure 1 and Figure 2 compare the calculated excess entropy of 2D OCPs (Coulomb and logarithmic) against existing Monte Carlo simulation data, demonstrating the model's accuracy.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the generalization of the vibrational model to 2D, derives the universal expression for $s_{ex}$, and then applies this framework to test its accuracy against known results for multiple 2D plasma and fluid models.
why_interesting: While focused on classical statistical mechanics, the rigorous application of vibrational models and the comparison of theoretical approximations (Einstein vs. DR-based) to simulation data mirrors the systematic theoretical development seen in many-body quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (Color online) Excess entropy of the 2D OCP with Coulomb (∝1/r) interaction versus the coupling parameter Γ. The solid line corresponds to a fit based on MC simula- tion results [44]. The dashed line corresponds to the present theoretical model.

FIG. 2. (Color online) Excess entropy of the 2D OCP with logarithmic interaction (∝ln(1/r)) versus the coupling pa- rameter Γ. The solid line corresponds to a fit based on MC and MD simulation results [20, 21]. The dashed line corre- sponds to the present theoretical model.

FIG. 3. (Color online) Excess entropy of the 2D fluid with dipole (∝1/r3) interaction versus the coupling parameter Γ. The solid line corresponds to a fit based on MD simulation results [29]. The dashed line corresponds to the present the- oretical model.

Figure 3 presents a comparison between theory and numerical data for a 2D fluid with the dipole-like inverse cube interaction potential. The estimate of the Einstein frequency at strong coupling can be made using the re- sults reported in Ref. [29]. Specifically, ω2 E ≃3

Figure 4 shows a comparison between the vibrational approximation to excess entropy and the numerical data for the 2D Yukawa fluid with κ = 1, 2, and 3. For the 2D Yukawa fluid, the ratio ωE/ω0 is only weakly dependent on Γ, but drops considerably as κ increases (see Fig. 4 from Ref. [42]). We use the values reported in Ref. [50], corresponding to the vicinity of the fluid-solid phase tran- sition: ωE/ω0 ≃0.5159 (κ = 1), ≃0.3297 (κ = 2), and ≃0.1893 (κ = 3). The agreement between the predic- tion of the vibrational model and the equation of state is again remarkably good at strong coupling.
